summary: Gruber reviews MKBHD's new Panels wallpaper app and finds it deeply flawed. He separates two strands of criticism: the dismissive 'wallpapers should be free' crowd, which he finds annoying, and the legitimate complaint that Panels is simply not a good app. He argues the app fails on nearly every front — poor UI, crashes, mediocre wallpapers, invasive tracking, and ads — while charging premium prices of $50/year. He contrasts it unfavorably with Iconfactory's Wallaroo, which costs less and delivers more. Gruber concludes that a premium wallpaper app could justify its price, but Panels is 'incongruous and incoherent' in charging premium while delivering sub-premium quality.
key_points: Criticism of Panels falls into two categories: cheapskates who think all wallpapers should be free, and valid criticism that the app itself is poor quality
The app's cross-platform UI looks native on neither iOS nor Android, crashes on first run, and feels more like a game interface than a premium app
Panels charges $50/year or $144/year monthly while tracking users, showing ads for free content, and having a poor privacy report card — all antithetical to a premium experience
The contrast between indie iOS community's warm reception of Wallaroo and mainstream tech's hostility toward Panels reveals how many people don't value original art
Brownlee's willingness to ship this app says something about his design sensibility and comfort with Android-level UI conventions
money_quotes: That second form of criticism — that there shouldn't even exist a paid wallpaper app — is annoying and depressing, and speaks to how many people do not view original art as worth paying for.
If one's argument is that all wallpapers ought to be free, that's not a valid starting point for criticism of a paid wallpaper app/service.
If Brownlee is down with how this app looks and feels, it explains quite a bit about how he's willing to spend large stretches of time daily-driving Android phones.
They feel like user-generated content, not professional content curated by a trusted tastemaker like Brownlee.
Premium apps don't ask to track you across apps. Premium apps don't make you watch ads to get to their free content. Premium apps look and feel native to their platforms.
As it stands, Panels is incongruous and incoherent.
It's entirely plausible for a premium wallpaper app to justify a price of $50/year. But Panels isn't a premium app.
key_insight: A premium price demands a premium experience across every dimension — design, privacy, content quality, and platform respect — and Panels fails on all of them.
